flashcards.front: What is exhaustive search (brute-force) in algorithm design?
flashcards.back: A fundamental technique that systematically examines ALL possible candidates for a solution and checks whether each candidate satisfies the problem's requirements. It searches through the entire solution space.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of brute-force linear search?
flashcards.back: O(n) - In the worst case, we examine every element once. Best case is O(1) if element is found at first position.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of bubble sort (brute-force sorting)?
flashcards.back: O(n²) time complexity. Best case O(n) when array is already sorted (with optimization), worst case O(n²) with n-1 passes and comparisons.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of the Subset Sum problem using exhaustive search?
flashcards.back: O(2^n) - We need to check all 2^n subsets of n elements to find if any subset sums to target value W.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of solving 0/1 Knapsack using brute-force?
flashcards.back: O(2^n) - We evaluate all possible subsets of n items (2^n combinations) to find the optimal combination that maximizes value within weight capacity.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of finding the optimal solution for Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) using brute-force?
flashcards.back: O(n!) - We must check all permutations of n cities to find the shortest Hamiltonian cycle. For 20 cities, this is approximately 2.4 × 10^18 operations.

flashcards.front: Why is brute-force often used as a baseline in algorithm design?
flashcards.back: It provides a simple, correct solution to compare against. If a more efficient algorithm fails, we can always fall back to brute-force for small inputs. It establishes the lower bound for correctness.

flashcards.front: What is backtracking in exhaustive search?
flashcards.back: An optimization where we abandon a partial solution as soon as it is determined to be infeasible, rather than completing the full search. Itprunes the search space.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of generating all permutations of n elements?
flashcards.back: O(n) for recursion stack. The total number of permutations is n!, but we only store one permutation at a time plus the recursion depth.

flashcards.front: When is brute-force/exhaustive search the preferred approach?
flashcards.back: When: (1) Problem size is small, (2) Simplicity matters more than efficiency, (3) No better algorithm exists, (4) As baseline for comparison, (5) Hardware is powerful enough.

flashcards.front: What is branch and bound in exhaustive search optimization?
flashcards.back: A technique that partitions the search space into branches and computes bounds to prune branches that cannot yield better solutions than already found. Used for optimization problems.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between exhaustive search and greedy algorithms?
flashcards.back: Exhaustive search checks ALL possibilities to guarantee optimal solution. Greedy makes locally optimal choices without exploring all options - faster but may not find global optimum.

flashcards.front: What is the search space size for checking all subsets?
flashcards.back: For n elements, there are 2^n possible subsets. This includes the empty set and the set containing all elements.

flashcards.front: Name 4 real-world problems solved by exhaustive search
flashcards.back: (1) Password cracking (2) Sudoku solving (3) Vehicle routing for small networks (4) Academic timetabling (5) Staff scheduling (6) Game solving (chess, sudoku)
